stories.headline: Mayor Johnston unveils universal childcare plan, 'Mile High Line' development vision in State of the City address
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston used his third annual State of the City address Tuesday to announce a plan for universal childcare and a sweeping redevelopment initiative he called the 'Mile High Line.' Johnston said a pilot childcare program would be available for testing by fall 2027, with a full rollout contingent on voter approval of a second term. He acknowledged that funding sources have not yet been determined, saying a task force would identify the cost and revenue structure over the next five years.

Johnston delivered the speech at the National Western Center's new Sue Anschutz-Rodgers Livestock Center in the Elyria-Swansea neighborhood. He shared the stage with City Councilwoman Shontel Lewis, who announced her mayoral bid last week, and called out newly elected congressional candidate Melat Kiros among other officials in attendance. Johnston said his childcare plan had been in development for months, predating Kiros's similar platform.

A centerpiece of the address was Johnston's vision for the 85-acre site currently occupied by the Denver Broncos' stadium, should the team proceed with its planned move to Burnham Yard by 2031. The mayor said the city would allow developers to build on the parcel without purchasing the land, with the goal of producing thousands of new housing units and new public spaces. A community planning process is expected to begin within the year.

stories.headline: AdventHealth and Intermountain Health announce joint venture covering eight Denver-area hospitals
stories.summary:
AdventHealth and Intermountain Health announced plans Tuesday to form a joint venture involving eight Denver-area hospitals, with the transaction expected to close in early 2027. Under the arrangement, AdventHealth will take over daily operations of Intermountain's Lutheran Hospital in Wheat Ridge, Platte Valley Hospital in Brighton, and Good Samaritan Hospital in Lafayette, along with associated clinics, freestanding emergency rooms, and urgent care centers.

The partnership represents a significant consolidation in the Denver metro healthcare market, where both systems already have a substantial presence. The deal would give AdventHealth operational control over a larger network of Front Range facilities at a time when health systems nationally are pursuing scale to manage cost pressures and reimbursement challenges.

Terms of the joint venture were not fully disclosed in Tuesday's announcement. The two systems said they expect the partnership to strengthen healthcare delivery for Denver metro communities, though regulatory review will be required before the transaction can close.

stories.headline: Colorado economy shedding jobs even as national economy grows, state launches competitiveness review
stories.summary:
Colorado's payroll employment has fallen by 6,200 jobs over the past 18 months, a troubling trend for a state accustomed to outperforming the national economy. The losses place Colorado 12th worst in total job losses and 14th worst in percentage job losses since January 2025, according to Bureau of Labor Statistics data, even as the broader U.S. economy continues to expand. The state's unemployment rate remains a relatively low 3.9%, but economists and officials say the trajectory is a warning sign.

Gov. Jared Polis plans to announce the formation of a new competitiveness council this week, involving public and private partners, to diagnose the problem and recommend policy responses. The Office of Economic Development and International Trade has also commissioned the University of Colorado Boulder's Leeds Business Research Division to analyze which economic indicators matter most and where Colorado holds competitive advantages. A series of regional listening sessions with businesses across all 64 counties is planned.

Officials at the Colorado Economic Development Commission cited high living costs, regulatory burdens, new workplace mandates, and infrastructure bottlenecks as concerns raised in early sessions. States including Nevada, Idaho, and Utah have posted the strongest job gains in the same period, heightening concern that Colorado is losing ground to regional competitors. The effort mirrors a similar outreach initiative conducted under former Gov. John Hickenlooper's Colorado Blueprint.

stories.headline: Front Range passenger rail plan takes shape: sales tax vote possible in November, limited service targeted for 2029
stories.summary:
Coloradans living within the Front Range Passenger Rail District could vote this November on a one-third cent sales tax that would generate $295 million annually to fund passenger rail service between Pueblo, Denver, Boulder, and Fort Collins. The rail board is expected to decide in late August whether to place the measure on the ballot. District general manager Sal Pace said a limited service line between Denver, Boulder, and Fort Collins could still launch in 2029 even without the tax, using existing state transportation funds and RTD money originally designated for unbuilt FasTracks lines.

The limited-service phase would operate three round trips daily on the Denver-to-Fort Collins corridor, with stops in Boulder and Longmont. That phase carries a $332 million price tag. Full buildout to Colorado Springs and Pueblo, branded the 'Colorado Connector' or CoCo, would cost $2.7 billion and take five years from a successful vote to complete, with Amtrak slated to operate the service.

Planners project the fully built system would attract 2,300 daily trips between Denver and Fort Collins. Notably, the rail agency is not positioning CoCo primarily as a commuter service — only 11 percent of expected ridership would be work-related, with the plurality of riders traveling for entertainment or sports events. The proposal comes as RTD continues to grapple with a 40 percent ridership decline among the 3 million residents of the Denver metro area.

stories.headline: Denver police arrest suspect in July 13 fatal shooting on West Arizona Avenue
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested Fernando Sebastian Chavez on Monday night in connection with a deadly shooting that occurred July 13 in the 3800 block of West Arizona Avenue, the department announced Tuesday. Officers identified Chavez as a suspect through the course of the investigation and took him into custody without further incident, according to a police press release.

Details about the victim and the circumstances surrounding the original shooting were not included in Tuesday's announcement. The arrest marks a development in a case that had remained unsolved for more than a week.

No additional suspects have been named publicly at this time. The investigation remains ongoing, according to the Denver Police Department.

stories.headline: Driver fleeing Commerce City police crashes into RTD FreeRide bus on 16th Street
stories.summary:
A pickup truck crashed into an RTD FreeRide bus at 16th and Champa streets in downtown Denver on Tuesday afternoon after a pursuit by Commerce City police. The chase began in Commerce City after officers spotted a passenger in the truck who was suspected in a prior shooting. Commerce City police called off the pursuit after losing sight of the vehicle, but the driver subsequently crashed into the bus.

The truck driver and passenger were both transported to a hospital. No passengers aboard the FreeRide bus were reported injured. RTD temporarily rerouted FreeRide service onto 15th and 17th streets while the scene was cleared.

The incident adds to ongoing scrutiny of the 16th Street corridor following recent discussions about the future of the free transit service. Commerce City police did not specify how much time elapsed between the end of the pursuit and the crash.

stories.headline: Monsoon moisture arrives in Colorado, bringing rain and flash flood risk after prolonged heat
stories.summary:
After an extended stretch of extreme heat and dry conditions, monsoonal moisture began moving into Colorado on Tuesday, offering the first meaningful chance of rain for the Denver metro area in weeks. The National Weather Service forecast a high near 94 degrees Tuesday with a 40 percent chance of rain into the overnight hours, with storms capable of producing locally heavy rainfall.

The weather service warned that storm coverage and intensity are expected to increase through Wednesday and Thursday as moisture continues to move into the region. Flash flooding is a concern, particularly in higher elevation areas and burn scars from recent wildfires across the state.

The arrival of monsoon moisture follows a period during which Denver recorded its first 100-degree temperature of 2026 and a heat advisory blanketed the Front Range. Temperatures are expected to drop significantly over the coming days as the storm pattern settles in.

stories.headline: Cannabist Co. closes Denver cannabis manufacturing facility, lays off 50 workers
stories.summary:
The Cannabist Co. is shutting down its Denver cannabis grow and manufacturing facility, cutting approximately 50 jobs as the company navigates bankruptcy proceedings and attempts to manage its debt load. The closure reflects the continuing collapse of wholesale cannabis prices in Colorado, which has squeezed margins across the industry and forced numerous operators to consolidate or exit the market.

Cannabist, formerly known as Columbia Care, has been restructuring its operations nationally as part of its bankruptcy process. The Denver facility closure is part of a broader effort to reduce overhead and focus resources on more profitable markets.

Colorado's cannabis industry has faced sustained pressure from oversupply and declining prices since the pandemic-era boom subsided. The Cannabist closure adds to a string of layoffs and business exits that have reshaped the state's legal marijuana sector in recent years.

stories.headline: Aldi files plans for first Thornton grocery store near I-25
stories.summary:
Aldi Real Estate Inc. has filed development plans for a 20,436-square-foot grocery store in Thornton near Interstate 25, marking one of the discount chain's first proposed locations in Colorado. The filing signals the German-owned grocer's continued push into new markets as it expands its U.S. footprint.

Aldi has grown rapidly across the country in recent years, competing on price against traditional supermarket chains. A Thornton location would bring its low-cost model to a rapidly growing northern Denver suburb that has seen significant residential and retail development along the I-25 corridor.

The project is in the planning stage, and a timeline for construction and opening has not been publicly announced. If approved, the store would be among the first Aldi locations to serve the Denver metro market.

stories.headline: Denver's original cannabis lounge Tetra to close RiNo location, plans to reopen at new site
stories.summary:
Tetra Lounge, one of Denver's pioneering cannabis consumption spaces, will close its River North Arts District location at 3039 Walnut St. on Aug. 10 when its lease expires. Opened in 2018, the business allowed guests to consume cannabis on-site and hosted events including joint-rolling classes. Owner Dewayne Benjamin said the business intends to reopen at a new, undisclosed location with an upgraded model.

The closure is partly driven by the limitations of the current building, which dates to 1921 and cannot support the infrastructure needed for modern cannabis hospitality operations. Changes to Denver's marijuana hospitality licensing framework also required Tetra to shift from an indoor private club model to an outdoor consumption garden in recent years, making a purpose-built space more desirable.

Tetra has had a complicated regulatory history, including a $10,000 fine earlier this year for allegedly allowing unlawful activity during events — allegations Benjamin has denied. The new location is intended to incorporate lessons learned from nearly a decade of operation and offer greater capacity for events and future growth. No opening date or address for the new venue has been announced.

stories.headline: Nuggets beat Raptors in NBA Summer League finale behind 20 points from Jahmi'us Ramsey
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets closed out their 2026 NBA Summer League schedule with a win over the Toronto Raptors, with guard Jahmi'us Ramsey leading the way with 20 points. The victory gave Denver a positive finish to its summer competition as the organization looks to evaluate younger players and roster candidates ahead of the regular season.

Summer League results carry no bearing on standings but are closely watched by teams assessing developmental players and potential roster additions. Ramsey's performance gives the Nuggets' front office additional data points as it navigates a busy offseason that includes the ongoing restricted free agency situation surrounding Peyton Watson.

Denver enters the fall with questions about depth and payroll flexibility after a playoff exit last spring. Training camp is expected to provide clearer answers about which young players are ready to contribute at the NBA level.

stories.headline: Broncos training camp approaches with new offensive coordinator Davis Webb and defensive line questions
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are preparing to open training camp with first-year offensive coordinator Davis Webb taking over the unit under head coach Sean Payton. Webb, who has no prior NFL coordinator experience, will have Payton as a direct resource as he installs his offensive system, a dynamic the team believes gives the rookie play-caller a significant advantage.

On defense, the Broncos face questions along the defensive line following the departure of defensive end Derek Barnett and the offseason exit of John Franklin-Myers. The team's ability to generate consistent pass rush without those contributors will be among the key storylines to watch when camp opens.

Denver's 2025 first-round draft pick is drawing attention as a potential contributor in the secondary this season. The Broncos held their annual media day recently, with several defensive linemen participating in team photo sessions ahead of the start of padded practice.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I've got nine kids. Well, five of them are mine. The other four, I don't know what happened.

topStory.headline: House Passes Stopgap Spending Bill to Avert Government Shutdown Through December 4
topStory.summary:
The U.S. House of Representatives passed a continuing resolution Tuesday night to extend federal government funding through December 4, averting a potential shutdown when current spending authority expires September 30. The measure passed largely along party lines by a vote of 220-205, with six Democrats voting in favor and only one Republican, Rep. Thomas Massie of Kentucky, voting against.

House Speaker Mike Johnson managed to hold his narrow majority together after weeks of internal Republican disputes, including a prolonged standoff over voter identification legislation. House Appropriations Committee Chair Tom Cole, who led efforts on the resolution, framed the early action as a preemptive move against what Republicans characterized as Democratic obstruction in the Senate. 'Our conference refuses to let the obstruction and inaction of Senate Democrats trigger another manufactured shutdown at the end of September,' Cole said.

House Democratic leaders opposed the measure, with Ranking Member Rosa DeLauro arguing it was 'hastily' conceived and objecting to the inclusion of border patrol funding without what she described as substantial reforms to Customs and Border Protection. Democrats also cited the bill's failure to block funding for CBP and ICE operations tied to the Trump administration's immigration enforcement agenda. DeLauro nonetheless acknowledged that a stopgap funding measure was 'necessary.'

The bill now moves to the Senate, where it will require bipartisan support to advance. Senate Majority Leader John Thune has indicated he is crafting his own version of a continuing resolution, and Senate Republican and Democratic leaders have described their positions on broader government spending as far apart. The Senate has passed none of the 12 annual appropriations measures required for the fiscal year beginning October 1, nor have any advanced through committee.

The outcome carries significance heading into the November midterm elections, with House Republican leaders signaling plans to highlight Democratic votes against the funding bill on the campaign trail. Senate Majority Leader Thune has also indicated he may withhold action on a separate reconciliation package until the government funding question is resolved, adding additional pressure to spending negotiations in the coming weeks.
